inflation.text: Inflation is roughly on target with expectations re-anchoring, even as recent monthly momentum has turned against the disinflationary trend. Headline CPI was 2.3% y/y in March; core measures sit in a tight band of 2.0% to 2.6%; breadth points to broad-based softening. Expectations are cooling on the business side — the share of firms expecting inflation above 3% has fallen to 11% (from 50%+ in late 2023) — and the consumer 5-year-ahead reading is down 0.4pp over the past year to 3.0%. The complication is March's +0.48% m/m print, well above the pace consistent with 2% annualized; the 3-month annualized rate is at 2.9%.

policy.text: The BoC has held rates at 2.25% for four meetings after a 275bp cutting cycle, and bond markets are starting to price the next move as more likely a hike than another cut. At 2.25%, the policy rate is right at the bottom of the BoC's 2.25-3.25% neutral range and 138 bps below where the Fed sits — an unusually wide gap, since the BoC cut more aggressively after both central banks peaked in July 2023. Canadian 2-year yields have risen over the past quarter and now trade 66 bps above the policy rate. The BoC finished QT in January 2025; total assets stand at $229B, and banks keep $71B in cash at the BoC overnight — slightly above the $20-60B steady-state target.

gdp.model: claude-opus-4-7 (autonomous draft, manual seed pending API key)
gdp.text: Headline Canadian GDP contracted 0.2% Q/Q annualized in the latest quarter, but the underlying picture is much stronger — final domestic demand contributed +2.3pp and net trade +2.1pp, with the entire drag from a -4.2pp inventory swing. Monthly GDP is still growing at +1.0% year-over-year as of February 2026, but the four-quarter average Q/Q AR of +0.2% is well below the BoC's central estimate of potential growth (~1.2-1.3% per April 2026 MPR). The BoC's most recent output gap estimate is -1.5% to -0.5% — the economy has been running below capacity even as growth is technically positive.

labour.model: claude-opus-4-7 (autonomous draft, manual seed pending API key)
labour.text: The Canadian labour market is softening; the unemployment rate sits at 6.70%, up 10 bps over the past three months and at the 70th percentile of its 5-year range. Wage growth is mixed: raw LFS measures run between 3.4% and 4.2%, but the BoC's composition-adjusted LFS-Micro reads at 2.60% — well below the raw average, the standard Canadian pattern when the workforce mix is shifting toward higher-paid workers. Real wages are positive at roughly 2 percentage points above headline inflation, and wage growth still runs above services CPI by over a percentage point — labour-cost pressures haven't fully passed through to services prices.

housing.model: claude-opus-4-7 (autonomous draft, manual seed pending API key)
housing.text: Canadian housing activity has picked up: starts hit 251,000 units annualized in the latest month with a 3-month average of 241,000, up 13% from a year ago — back into the recent historical range but still well below CMHC's estimated affordability-restoring pace of roughly 430-500k/year. New-home prices are still declining year-over-year (NHPI -2.1%), though the monthly print is mildly positive at +0.3%; note NHPI excludes condo apartments and resale. Residential building permits are 2.8% below year-ago levels — the leading-indicator pipeline is modestly weakening even as current starts strengthen, a divergence worth watching given the 9-15 month lag for the multi-unit-dominant Canadian housing mix.

financial.model: claude-opus-4-7 (autonomous draft, manual seed pending API key)
financial.text: Oil is the dominant inflation impulse right now — WTI is up 74% over the past year (63% in just the last three months) to $105/bbl, mechanically adding about 2.7 percentage points to headline CPI through gasoline. CAD is roughly flat against USD over the past year (USDCAD at 1.3575, down 2%), giving a slight cooling effect on goods CPI rather than a meaningful offset to oil. The petrocurrency relationship is weak post-2016: oil is up sharply while CAD barely moved, with daily-change correlation at +0.10 over the year. WCS is at an unusual $9 premium to WTI, inverting the typical $10-15 discount.
